_meta._comment: Hand-curated EN/JP gloss backfill for compounds inherited from Tommy 1949 and the Japanese Wiktionary. Keys are lemmas; values may be a string (single English gloss), an array of English glosses, or {en: [...], jp: [...]}. Translations are concise renderings of the Japanese source glosses (Tommy 1949 / Kayano / Tamura), not new lexicographic decisions. Entries that already have a gloss in the database are not overwritten.

kamuynukar: be watched over by the gods
nukarewen: be hard to see; see poorly

en: offer a sacrifice to the sea
make unable to wield a sword
upstream-side of the hearth (seat of honour)
arrows set up in a row

akimokar: be killed by a bear on the mountain
apehosi: turn one's back to the fire
apepiro: be burned by a flying spark
aynueham: lodge a person at one's house
aynutura: be in company with a person
ay-uk: be hit by an arrow; take an arrow in hand
cawcawatki: sound of large bird wings; flash sharply
cepnu: have a big catch of fish
cinkanroski: lie on one's back with feet up
cipkoyanke: haul up together with the boat
ciposirkosak: the boat runs aground on a shoal
ciprepo: put a boat out to sea
cisepa: the far side of the house
cisepuni: raise a house onto its posts
cuppusu: extract a foetus after the mother's death
eattamneno: with one stroke of the sword
ecipo: load up and board the boat
ehontomne: be half-done; be in the middle
ehorkaapkas: walk on one's hands; walk upside-down
ekimnerusuy: want to hunt in the mountains
emateniwkes: be unable to take a wife
emoykokarke: be swept around in a whirlpool
eoci: place to be taken care of
eorhankeko: what a long way to come!
eparura: pass word from mouth to mouth
esauturnu: have a gap between the front teeth
esiseturka-terkere: carry a baby on one's back inside the garment
esumkus: belch from eating too much oil
etanpaun: to this side of the river
etutope: below the bridge of the nose
eyaynunuke: be unable to bring oneself to
eyayopokin: do one thing after another oneself
eypottumma: from the look on one's face
honkakar: have intercourse with a pregnant woman
hoturihoturi: pull at the waist and feet
howesara: open one's chest wide; reveal one's feelings
iopes: keep asking the same thing in old age
iperamante: visit at mealtime to scrounge food
irieaskay: be able to skin a hide
isirkootke: be pierced together with the earth
itakturasire: send words crawling up from the lower seat
itanki-arke-e: share half a bowl with a woman as a marriage proposal
itomunpuyar: the third window from the entrance (light window)
iwor-ukaeraye: take a shortcut over the ridge
iyaepas: chase along the bank what is drifting in the river
iyemateniwkes: have not yet taken a wife
kamuyhopunire: send a slain bear's spirit on its way
kamuykar-onne: die at the time decreed by the gods
kamuykoypuni: offer food to the bear god
kamuykoyukar: epic to be heard by the bear god
kamuysapaunni: wood that holds the bear skull
kasutar: people who live in fishing huts
kimokere: walk every part of the mountains
koekari: do at the same moment as
koitakmuye: leave a request or order behind
kokam-ahupte: bring the meat of game inside
koraynatara: be on the verge of collapse
kotankarkamuy: god who made the Ainu homeland
kotannonnoytak: cast a curse on a village
koureyupu: grip the hearth-edge with one's feet
